coredata.dc:title: Deep learning-assisted multicolor fluorescent probes for image and spectral dual-modal identification of illicit drugs
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Rui
Zhou, Yi
Hu, Jiaxiang
Peng, An
Hu, Weihao
coredata.dc:description: llicit drug detection is an important part of drug problem management, and existing detection strategies suffer from inefficiency and narrow scope of application, which cannot fully meet the needs of practical work. Here, a multi-color fluorescent carbon dots was synthesized in one step and used as probes for the fluorescence sensing detection of three habitual illicit drugs, heroin, ketamine and methamphetamine. The fluorescence response results were recorded in the form of image fixation and spectral detection, which contained the subtle fingerprint information of these illicit drugs. Further, a multi-modal detector based on multi-task deep learning was constructed to achieve qualitative and semi-quantitative illicit drug detection, with accuracies of 98.4 % and 84.4 % for image and 99.9 % and 99.6 % for spectral, respectively, and a broad detection range from 1 ng/mL to 1 mg/mL. The process showed that deep learning significantly enhanced the detection sensitivity and range of fluorescent probes, and the dual-modal image-spectrum recognition met the practical needs of illicit drug on-site fast detection and accurate analysis, providing a new technical solution for illicit drug detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor fluorescent probes
Deep learning
Illicit drugs
Dual-modal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of MFCDs and illicit drugs
Multi-task deep learning qualitative and semi-quantitative recognition
Fluorescence response of MFCDs and illicit drugs
Deep learning-assisted qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis
Simulated crime scene urine interference analysis

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 62002301 ); Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing , China ( 2022NSCQ-LZX0326 ); Chongqing Municipal Education Commission Key Project ( KJZD-M202300301 , KJZD-K202200302 , KJQN201900310 ); 2022 Chongqing Postgraduate Research Innovation Project ( CYS22307 ); Chongqing Innovative Research Group ( CXQT21033 ); Southwest University of Political Science and Law Research Project ( 2018XZQN-31 , 2018XZQN-33 , 2021XZNDJDZD-13 , 2022-XZWTYB04 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart of dual-modal identification of illicit drugs by MFCDs and Deep learning.

Characterization of MFCDs. (A) HR-TEM (a), TEM (b) and Particle size distribution (c) of MFCDs. (B) FL spectra (a), UV–vis absorption spectra (b) and fluorescence aggregation burst spectra (c). (C) Structural characterization of MFCDs. (a) XPS characterization; (b∼d) XPS elemental distribution.

Fluorescence response of MFCDs with illicit drugs. (A) Fluorescence spectra data fingerprints of MFCDs after adding heroin (a), ketamine (b), methamphetamine (c) and equivalent amount of pure water (d). (B) Corresponding image data fingerprints. (C) Comparison of the fluorescence response of MFCDs with three illicit drugs and common metal ions. (D) Effect of illicit drugs on the fluorescence peak of MFCDs corresponding to (A).

Deep learning-assisted illicit drug detection. (A) CNN-FCN multimodal qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis model. (B) Training process of image and spectral data. (a, b) Qualitative and semi-quantitative process of image data, (c, d) Spectral data. (C, D) Confusion matrix, Qualitative and semi-quantitative results (t-SNE downscaled plots) corresponding to (B).

Fluorescence response of MFCDs with illicit drugs under the interference of AU. (a) Spectral and image data of MFCDs before adding urine. (b) Spectral and image data of MFCDs after adding urine. (c, d, e) The data after adding urine and heroin, ketamine, methamphetamine (1 ng/mL).

Deep learning-assisted illicit drug detection under the interference of AU. (A) Confusion matrix of qualitative and semi-quantitative process of image data (a, b), Spectral data (c, d). (B) t-SNE downscaled plots corresponding to (A).

row.entry:
The structure of each layer
(feeling range，filter number)
Residual convolution block ((3, 2), 64）/ Residual convolution block ((3, 3), 64）

head.ce:title: Deep learning-assisted multicolor fluorescent probes for image and spectral dual-modal identification of illicit drugs

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Criminal Investigation, Southwest University of Political Science and Law, Chongqing 401120, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Criminal Investigation, Southwest University of Political Science and Law

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: llicit drug detection is an important part of drug problem management, and existing detection strategies suffer from inefficiency and narrow scope of application, which cannot fully meet the needs of practical work. Here, a multi-color fluorescent carbon dots was synthesized in one step and used as probes for the fluorescence sensing detection of three habitual illicit drugs, heroin, ketamine and methamphetamine. The fluorescence response results were recorded in the form of image fixation and spectral detection, which contained the subtle fingerprint information of these illicit drugs. Further, a multi-modal detector based on multi-task deep learning was constructed to achieve qualitative and semi-quantitative illicit drug detection, with accuracies of 98.4 % and 84.4 % for image and 99.9 % and 99.6 % for spectral, respectively, and a broad detection range from 1 ng/mL to 1 mg/mL. The process showed that deep learning significantly enhanced the detection sensitivity and range of fluorescent probes, and the dual-modal image-spectrum recognition met the practical needs of illicit drug on-site fast detection and accurate analysis, providing a new technical solution for illicit drug detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Drugs can kill. The world post-pandemic (COVID-19) remains one in crisis, and the world drug challenges further complicate the picture [1]. Promoting the front of illicit drug detection (at the end of the anti-drug work chain) to achieve early detection and early combat of illicit drug cases will strongly promote the construction of a large-scale census of illicit drug crimes and an early warning mechanism, and realize the anti-client orientation of anti-drug work. Currently, illicit drug detection methods mainly include mass spectrometry, chromatography, spectroscopy, electrochemical methods, immunoassay methods, etc [2–7]. Most of these methods rely on precision instruments and have high thresholds, which are not conducive to the rapid detection of illicit drugs seized at the scene by the grassroots level of public security, and most of the existing rapid detection methods are "one drug, one test", which has low efficiency and low utilization of evidence, limiting the effect of illicit drug detection in the whole chain of anti-drug work, Therefore, there is a need to establish a low-threshold, high-efficiency illicit drug detection technology.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in biomedical imaging, sensing and detection, catalysis and optical devices due to their simple preparation, excellent fluorescence, optical stability, biocompatibility/low toxicity, and ease of modification [8–11]. Since CDs are mostly chemically inert, they are usually used in illicit drug detection by combining with antibodies, aptamers, and molecular blots to achieve the detection of targets [7,12–14]. However, the drawback of this strategy is that only a single/small number of targets can be recognized and the preparation process is complicated. Therefore, providing active sites for CDs by atomic doping [15,16], and reacting them directly with the target became another strategy [17–19]. For example, in 2019, Yen et al. achieved the detection of three illicit drugs, 4-chloroethylcathinone, cocaine and heroin, by this strategy [17]. This not only simplified the material synthesis process but also improved the detectability of materials. Still, there are many aspects need to be improved, such as the fluorescence properties of the material, the sensitivity of the detection and the analysis of complex fluorescence data, which are key to solving the problems in practice.

In fact, the high dimensionality, complexity, and nonlinearity in chemistry are particularly well suited to be solved and predicted by data-driven artificial intelligence algorithms [20]. In recent years, with the development of computer science and information technology, deep learning techniques have become important tools in various fields [20–22], and they also provided new strategies for research in chemistry disciplines. In 2018, He Yao's group at Soochow University reported for the first time a deep learning-assisted surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) approach to distinguish tumor suppressor genes in a label-free manner, pioneering AI-based sensing applications [23]. Deep learning can quantitatively verify known physical laws and explore unknown intrinsic connections at the atomic level with sufficient samples. In 2019, Ju L et al. used deep learning to capture the fingerprint information of different illicit drugs from the 3D fluorescence difference spectra of drugs and silver nanoclusters [24]. This study was the first to use deep learning for illicit drug detection with a wide detection range (0.002–100 mg/mL) and high accuracy. In 2022, Tan et al. used deep learning-assisted visual fluorescence array sensing to achieve rapid identification of six antibiotics by learning 8496 different fluorescence images [25]. The spectral data obtained by the instrument ensures the accuracy of the detection, while the visualization images induced by the fluorescent probe are more easily obtained, which is more conducive to the on-site fast detection of illicit drugs.

Inspired by these, this work takes three habitual illicit drugs, heroin, ketamine and methamphetamine, which are frequently abused in practical work, as a breakthrough. Firstly, we synthesized multi-color fluorescent carbon dots (MFCDs) by nitrogen doping, making them equipped with sufficient active sites to be used as probes for fluorescence response with the drugs. The response results were recorded in a dual-modal "image-spectral" mode. Further, a multi-modal detector (MMD) based on multi-task deep learning are constructed to mine the fingerprint information of different illicit drug molecules from images and spectra respectively. And the three illicit drugs are identified and quantified by the "image-spectral" dual-modal approach eventually. The overall process is shown in Fig. 1. The "image-spectral" dual-modality detection combines operability and accuracy, portability and precision, providing a more convenient and efficient solution for illicit drug detection in anti-drug work.

Ammonium citrate (C6H17N3O7) and urea (H2NCONH2) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Heroin, ketamine, and methamphetamine are provided by the Chongqing Public Security Bureau's Anti-Narcotics Headquarters. All chemical reagents were analytically pure, and the water used in the experiments was ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm).

BK-360B ultrasonic instrument (Jinan), DZF-6020 vacuum drying oven (Shanghai) were used for synthesis. Shimadzu RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (FL, Japan), Analytik Jena SPECORD 200 PLUS UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Germany), ThermoFisher Talos F200S field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, Czech Republic), ThermoFisher Nicolet iS50 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR, USA), and ThermoFisher ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS, UK) were used for characterization. Other devices included Milli-Q ultrapure water machine (USA), ESJ200-4B analytical balance (Shenyang), A021 FGGY-IVA multi-band light source (Beijing).

All calculations and deep learning methods were done in Pycharm based on the Python language. All programs run on Windows 10 Professional (64-bit). All neural networks are built by calling the Pytorch API in Pycharm. The CPU is i7–11700k, the GPU is RTX3080, and the deep learning framework is built using Pytorch.

The MFCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. First, 0.2 g of ammonium citrate was dissolved in 25 mL of distilled water, to which 0.2 g of urea was added, stirred well and sonicated for 2 min, and the solution was clarified and transparent, then transferred to 40 mL of PTFE liner and placed in a reaction kettle at 180 °C for 5 h. After it cooled to room temperature, the reaction solution was filtered through a needle filter (0.22 µm) The larger particles were removed by filtration, followed by dialysis (500 Da) of the reaction solution for 6 h to remove the unreacted residual compounds. Finally, the resulting CDs solution was refrigerated at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of MFCDs and illicit drugs
ce:section.ce:para: The original MFCDs solution was diluted to different degrees, and the diluted MFCDs solution was mixed with the prepared illicit drug samples at a volume ratio of 5:1. Each sample contained one illicit drug, and each drug contained five concentration gradients: 0 mg/mL, 1 × 10−6 mg/mL (1 ng/mL), 1 × 10−4 mg/mL, 1 × 10−2 mg/mL, and 1 × 100 mg/mL. Another MFCDs solution was mixed with pure water in the same volume ratio as a reference. After mixing for 5 min, the fluorescence images and spectral data of the mixed solutions were recorded at four excitation bands of 365 nm, 415 nm, 450 nm and 510 nm. (Please refer to the supplementary information for details and reasons.) The above experiments were repeated and validated five times, and 65 samples were taken for each experiment.

The image data and spectral data obtained were used to construct the original dataset required for the deep learning model. The original image data and spectral data contained three classifications including heroin, ketamine, and methamphetamine. Each classification had four concentration gradients with the addition of blank samples, the qualitative had four categories and the semi-quantitative had five categories. Heroin, ketamine and methamphetamine had 5 replicate experimental samples at each concentration gradient, and the original spectral data samples used for deep learning model were 65 for a single experiment. The image data were similar to the spectral data. The sample space of the depth model was constructed by data enhancement. The image information dataset of 650 image samples is obtained by randomly rotating, inverting, and cropping operations repeated 10 times on the image data. The original length of 376 spectral data samples are downsampled with a sampling window length of 112 and a sampling step of 1. This downsampling method increases the number of samples and places higher demands on the deep learning model. The model only needs to input part of the spectral data to complete the detection task. By downsampling, the number of spectral information samples is expanded to 65 * (376−112) = 17160. Both the image data set and the spectral data set correspond to 4 types of qualitative data and 5 types of semi-quantitative data. 70 % of the two data sets are used as training data sets and the remaining 30 % as test data sets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-task deep learning qualitative and semi-quantitative recognition
ce:section.ce:para:
In this work, qualitative and quantitative classifiers are constructed using deep learning-based models. The complete feature extraction of image and spectral data information is achieved by proposed MMD, which is established through special residual convolutional block, Full Connect Network (FCN) and combined feature processing units. Specifically, the core of convolutional neural network (CNN)is to scan the data information by convolutional kernel [26]. The convolution kernel is computed as in Eq. (1):
(1)
conv
x , y
=
∑
i
p * q
w
i
v
i
where
( x ， y )
is the input feature size of the spectral waveform,
p * q
is the size of the convolution kernel, w is the weight of the convolution kernel, and v is the waveform feature. The convolution process is the inner product of the convolution kernel and the input waveform features. Each convolution kernel records the waveform features of the sample by gradient propagation with supervised training guidance. Since CNNs are translation invariant, key features can be detected and extracted by CNNs regardless of their position in the sample. CNNs can extract detailed features of input information with spatial correlation and convert this information into feature encoding in high-dimensional space. The FCN is the task output layer, which analyzes the features extracted by the CNN with respect to the task type. The model has two outputs in parallel, a deterministic feature and a quantitative feature. The output layers of the multi-task classifier all use the softmax function as the activation function, as shown in Eq. (2).
(2)
softmax
Z
i
=
e
Z
i
∑
c = 1
C
e
Z
c
where
softmax (
Z
i
)
denotes the probabilistic output of the category,
Z
i
is the output of the neuron, and C is the number of categories in the output layer. Thus, the output threshold of the neural network can be adjusted to suit different tasks.

The morphology, size and fluorescence properties of the samples were characterized by high-resolution TEM, fluorescence and violet spectrophotometer. Fig. 2Ab shows that MFCDs are quasi-spherical and well dispersed, and its local magnification (Fig. 2 Aa) shows the high-resolution TEM results of the sample, which exhibits clear lattice stripes with a planar spacing of 0.31 nm. This is consistent with the (002) plane of g-C3N4 [27], proving the successful synthesis of MFCDs. Fig. 2 Ac shows that the average particle size of MFCDs is about 8.6 nm. Fig. 2 Ba shows that under excitation at 400 nm excitation wavelength, the MFCDs original solution has the highest emission peak at 441 nm with blue fluorescence, and with the increase of excitation wavelength, there is a significant red shift and a significant weakening of the emission wavelength.

To investigate this, UV–vis characterization was first performed for the original and diluted solutions of MFCDs, respectively. In Fig. 2 Bb, curve 1 (dilute solution) shows characteristic absorption peaks at 234 and 344 nm, which is consistent with that reported by Zhao et al. [28] This is attributed to the π-π * electron leap of sp2-hybridized CC and CN bonds in polymeric carbon-nitrides containing tri-s-trazine rings (C-N = C) [29,30] and the presence of the n→π * transition of CO and CN bonds in g-C3N4 [31]; the absorbance of curve 2 before 400 nm is similar to curve 1, while the difference is that after 400 nm it shows a decreasing and then stable absorbance. This phenomenon is similar to related studies, but with different principles, such as the multicolor fluorescence of MFCDs prepared by Zhu et al. after 400 nm is determined by different chemical bonds on the surface of carbon dots, and their UV–vis absorption spectra are not affected by concentration changes [32]; the broadening of the fluorescence peaks and long tails toward higher wavelengths of carbon dots prepared by Tao et al. are due to the presence of phosphorescence [33]. And upon investigation, the multicolor fluorescence of MFCDs in this work showed a high correlation with the concentration (Fig. 2 Bc).

In Fig. 2 Bc, the highest emission intensity (blue fluorescence at 440 nm) increases and then decreases with the dilution of the original solution of MFCDs, which is consistent with the fluorescence internal filtration effect [34]. In contrast, in Fig. S1, the emission intensity of other colors of fluorescence such as at 465 nm, 527 nm, and 586 nm decreased continuously with the dilution of MFCDs solution. That is, the MFCDs solution showed multicolor fluorescence at high concentrations, while only blue fluorescence was observed at low concentrations. The aggregated state (high concentration) of the red shift can be attributed to the particle aggregation-induced generation of Förster resonance energy transfer, which drives the overlap of emission spectra in the short wavelength region while leading to the relative enhancement at longer wavelengths [35]. This phenomenon is also similar to the aggregation-induced luminescence effect, where the energy gap in the aggregated state is narrowed due to the newly created spatial conjugation, resulting in a wavelength redshift [36].

The surface elemental composition and valence states of MFCDs were further analyzed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2Ca, the full spectral scan of MFCDs shows the photoelectron spectral lines attributed to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. In the extended XPS spectra, the C 1 s peaks at 284.6, 285.2, 287.3, and 289.0 eV shown in Fig. 2Cb can be assigned to carbon as C-C (sp3), C-N (sp3), CO (sp2), and O-CO (sp2), respectively [37,38]. The N 1 s peaks at 400.2 and 401.7 eV shown in Fig. 2 Cc indicate that the nitrogen is mainly present in the form of (C)3-N (sp3) and N-H (sp3) [39], which proves that the synthesized MFCDs are nitrogen-doped CDs. The O 1 s peaks at 531.8 and 533.3 eV shown in Fig. 2 Cd are associated with CO and C-OH /C-O-C states of oxygen correlation [40]. The results of XPS experiments fully demonstrate the successful synthesis of MFCDs and the presence of a large number of reactive chemical bonds on their surfaces, providing sufficient active sites for their use in illicit drug detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of MFCDs and illicit drugs
ce:section.ce:para: In the solution system, there are different interactions between different illicit drugs and MFCDs, so the overall fluorescence of the system will change differently depending on the illicit drugs. The fluorescence changes at 365, 415,450 and 510 nm were recorded, and the sum of the image data at the four bands constituted an image sample, and the same applies to the spectral data. (Fig. S2). These changes are included in the image and spectral data, and the combination of the data changes in different bands constitutes the fingerprint information of the illicit drugs (Heroin, Ketamine and Methamphetamine) ( Fig. 3A,B). More details of Fig. 3B shows in Fig. S5. The effects of the three illicit drugs on the fluorescence of MFCDs are reflected in the image samples with certain variations, but difficult to distinguish with the naked eye, and in the spectral data in the variation of the peaks at different bands (Fig. 3D), the position and shape of the spectra also have subtle effects (Fig. S3), which is consistent with previous studies [24,25].

Given the weak response between illicit drugs and MFCDs, we further examined the fluorescence response between some common metal ions (Sb3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Al3+, K+) and MFCDs. As shown in Fig. 3C, Sb3+, Cu2+, Mg2+ had the strongest effect on MFCDs, followed by Pb2+ and Al3+. It can be seen that most of the metal ions respond to MFCDs more strongly than these three illicit drug molecules. At this point, the necessity and advantage of deep learning in analyzing such weak response data is profoundly demonstrated. The addition of deep learning enables the detection of even weakly responding targets, compared to the traditional detection of targets with obvious responses or specific detection.

Meanwhile, it can be visualized from Fig. 3C that the fluorescence intensity of MFCDs was enhanced and diminished simultaneously with the addition of the three illicit drug molecules. Compared to the single fluorescence enhancement or quenching in existing studies, this phenomenon is relatively complex [41]. The interaction between illicit drug molecules and MFCDs may be due to a complex response between the reactive groups (amine, carbonyl, etc.) on drug molecules and those on MFCDs (hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, etc.), thus changing the intermolecular energy gap (Fig. S5). However, the mechanism of interaction between illicit drugs and MFCDs is still not well explored. Without knowing the response mechanism, the powerful nonlinear analysis capability of deep learning is directly utilized to establish the mapping relationship between image-spectral data and the type/concentration of illicit drugs by using data as input and type/concentration as output, respectively. The strategy of fuzzy identification allows a good qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis of illicit drugs, echoing the study of Ju L et al. [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deep learning-assisted qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In order to simultaneously meet the practical needs of both on-site and laboratory precision illicit drug detection, this work adopts a dual-modality image-spectral strategy, with illicit drug detection divided into two paths: image sensing and spectral sensing (Fig. 1). The "image sensing" only requires two small and portable devices: Portable multi-band light source and smartphone. The multi-band light source provides different excitation wavelengths, and the cell phone captures the corresponding emission wavelengths, and then the images are input into the qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis model to realize the rapid detection of illicit drugs. The "spectral sensing" is to obtain the fluorescence response data between illicit drug molecules and MFCDs through large instruments such as fluorescence spectrometer, to conduct more accurate qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis.

Thanks to the adaptive feature extraction through gradient backpropagation [42] which can avoid human interference as much as possible. The deep learning-based MMD model can effectively extract the joint features from the multi-modal information, which is owing to the adaptive feature extraction through gradient backpropagation [42]. This enables the deep learning technology to avoid human interference from the feature extraction process. For the limitation of weak fluorescence response between MFCDs and illicit drugs in part 3.2, this work uses CNN as the main feature extraction module and FCN as the task-related discriminant module with the powerful feature extraction capability of CNN. The proposed multimodal analysis model is shown in Fig. 4A, and the detail parameters of this model are shown in Table 1.

The representational capacity of deep learning models is a fundamental requirement for supporting illicit drug detection tasks. To improve the performance and accuracy of detection models, a specialized residual convolution block structure has been designed to enhance the representational capacity of proposed MMD. Considering that the input feature of the original l-th layer is
H
l
, the proposed residual convolution block is shown as follows:
(3)
H
l + 1
= φ
Relu
h
c 1
l + 1
+
h
c 2
l + 1
+
h
c 3
l + 1
(4)
h
c 1
l + 1
= Sigmoid
H
l
*
y
c 1
l + 1
(5)
h
c 2
l + 1
= φ
H
l
*
y
c 2
l + 1
(6)
h
c 3
l + 1
=
H
l
*
y
c 3
l + 1
where
H
l + 1
represents the output feature of the l+ 1-th layer, and
y
c 1
l + 1
，
y
c 2
l + 1
，
y
c 3
l + 1
represent three convolutional kernels. The symbol φ denotes batch normalization operation. The l+ 1-th layer feature
H
l + 1
is obtained by adding the results of three convolution operations and applying the ReLU activation function. This structure can extract features of the original information from multiple perspectives and effectively enhance the capacity of deep learning models. It can be regarded as a multi-channel feature extraction process.

The deep learning-based MMD model proposed in this work has 17 hidden layers and 12-output layers are multi-task output modules, which serve to apply the key features extracted from layers 1–11 to two different tasks, i.e., qualitative and quantitative analysis. With this model, the type and concentration of illicit drugs can be directly detected by the model simultaneously. Also, for the input spectral data, the horizontal and vertical coordinates are the number of wavelengths and the excitation intensity, respectively. Therefore, a convolution kernel of (3, 2) with stride = (1,2) is used to process this type of data. With this convolution kernel, the model can effectively use the correlation information between the number of wavelengths and the excitation intensity in the spectrum. When the input is image data, the whole image is scanned using the (3, 3), stride = (1,1) convolution kernel to obtain detailed spatial feature information.

The training process of the model for the image data is shown in Fig. 4B (a, b). it can be seen from the figure that the data enhancement can improve the performance of the model through increasing the diversity of input feature. Even though the original image dataset is small and the feature differences between different categories are relatively small compared with spectral data, the model still has valid performance for a fast detection (Fig. S6). The image information classifier achieves 98.4 % and 84.4 % for qualitative and quantitative tasks on the test set, respectively. This indicates that the model can give an effective primary detection result by taking pictures with image detection. The training process of the model for spectral data is shown in Fig. 4B (c, d), and it can be seen from the figure that the model has a strong fitting ability and can effectively construct the mapping relationship between the substance spectral data and the substance type and concentration. The model achieves 99.9 % and 99.6 % qualitative and quantitative accuracy on the test set, respectively. This indicates that the model can give accurate results of substance analysis by spectral data. Table S1 and Fig. 4C correspond to the numbering and tester confusion matrix for both data inputs.

From the confusion matrix in Fig. 4C, its target type class means the true type of samples. Its output type class means the predicted type by network. Therefore, the value of diagonal means the right prediction of networks. And the value of diagonal means the wrong prediction of networks. According to these four sub-figures of Fig. 4C, almost all value which located at position of non-diagonal lines is zero, which means proposed method can effectively accomplish the task of illicit drug type and concentration analysis. In order to further test the performance of the model, the output of the penultimate layer of the model is taken out and reduced in dimensionality by the t-SNE algorithm in this work (Fig. 4D). The output layer of the depth model is generally related to the number of task categories, while the penultimate layer is a vector feature closely related to the task. With the t-SNE dimensionality reduction, the output of the model for different samples can be appreciably displayed on the two-dimensional coordinate plane. All points in t-SNE downscaled plots represents samples in test dataset. If the network has capacity of distinguishing these samples theoretically, the samples belong to same class should located at same area. In this work, the performance of the model is analyzed in this way.

Fig. 4D shows the t-SNE dimensionality reduction for image input and spectral input and two tasks. It can be seen from the Fig. 4Da that the image information of different location types are reasonably classified into four types after passing through the deep learning model. The classifier ground effect is stronger. It can be concluded that the deep learning model proposed in this work can effectively analyze the material types by image data. It can be seen from Fig. 4 Db that the image data of different concentrations can be roughly separated after the model. However, the effectiveness of this classifier is limited due to the weak features and the close distance of different types. As shown in Fig. 4 D (c, d), the spectral data are fully formed into different feature vectors by output category after the model. The model proposed in this work can effectively process the spectral data and carry out the task of illicit drug type and concentration analysis, which can meet the dual practical needs of on-site fast inspection by image data and laboratory fine inspection by spectral data. Compared with the materials, models, and methods used in several other related studies [14,24,25], the advantages of this work are shown in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulated crime scene urine interference analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, illicit drugs need to be detected in two situations. First, rapid scanning for solid drugs in public areas is needed to prevent drug trafficking and transport [43]. Second, the identification of drug addicted in the suspected population by analyzing their biological fluids (e.g., urine, saliva) is needed [24,44]. Here, we further simulate the detection of illicit drugs in crime scene urine with artificial urine (AU).

AU composition (g/L) [45] is Na2HPO4·12 H2O 2.1896, NaCl 6.7725, NH4Cl 2.3215, Na2SO4 2.4310, KCl 6.0650, MgSO4 0.3572, CaCl2 0.6664, Na3C6H5O7 0.9440, NaH2PO4·2 H2O 1.3300. Consider it as a whole and mix it with MFCDs and pure water in the ratio of 4:1:1 (MFCDs:AU:water) volume ratio. Then, MFCDs was mixed with AU and illicit drugs in the 4:1:1 vol ratio in response. The concentration of each illicit drug was divided into three gradients (1 mg/mL, 1 ug/mL and 1 ng/mL). Each set of fluorescence responses was repeated 5 times and 65 samples (images, spectral samples) were taken for each response. The samples were further enhanced and classified by the method in part 2.3 and finally input the model for analysis.

As shown in Fig. 5(a, b), the fluorescence of MFCDs produced a strong quenching after the addition of AU. This is due to the metal cations (Mg2+, Ca2+and Na+, etc.) in AU react with oxygen surface groups on the carbon dots to form complexes [46]. With further addition of illicit drugs in the presence of AU, it can be seen from Fig. 5(c,d,e) that the effect of the three drug molecules on the carbon dots is further reduced and their fluorescence changes are difficult to distinguish with the naked eye. This is most likely due to strong binding affinity and faster chelating kinetics between the ions in AU and surface defects of MFCDs larger than the three illicit drug molecules [47]. Usually, in such cases, it is the ions in AU that can be detected, not the drugs in this work.

However, the experiments proved that the proposed model can analyze different drug types and concentrations under the strong interference of AU. The training process of image and spectral data is shown in Fig. S7, the corresponding Confusion matrix, Qualitative and semi-quantitative results (t-SNE downscaled plots) are shown in Fig. 6. Compared to Fig. 4C, the results of Fig. 6A shows slightly more incorrect predictions. Nevertheless, most of the values located at non-diagonal lines are still zero, which means that the proposed method achieves a good prediction. According to Fig. 6B, the points with same color and shape form a cluster, which has enough distance with other clusters to distinguish these samples.

Overall, consistent with the previous conclusions reached in Part 3.3, the CNN-FCN model proposed in this work can effectively handle image and spectral data for drug type and concentration classification even though the interference of AU is great. This further validates that deep learning can significantly enhance the detection sensitivity and range of fluorescent probes.

In brief, MFCDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method using ammonium citrate and urea as the carbon and nitrogen sources. The nitrogen doping provides abundant active sites on the surface of the MFCDs, which are useful for fluorescence sensing of illicit drugs. The "image-spectral" dual-modal dataset is built to meet the dual practical needs of illicit drug on-site fast detection and precise detection in the laboratory. The deep learning models built by CNN and FCN and various feature processing units provide high accuracy for qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis of three illicit drugs. With the assistance of deep learning, those targets that respond even weakly with the sensing material can be detected accurately. This enhances the sensable range of sensing materials (quantitative range for single target and detection variety for multiple targets), which can save a lot of time for researchers to screen sensing materials. However, in this case, it is more difficult to explore the sensing mechanism between the sensing material and the target. And it is worthwhile to continue to explore in depth how to exclude interference to achieve accurate detection of weakly responsive targets when there exist interfering substances that respond strongly to the sensing material. Deep learning may lead to more surprises.

Rui Huang: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Yi Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jiaxiang Hu: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. An Peng: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Weihao Hu: Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62002301); Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (2022NSCQ-LZX0326); Chongqing Municipal Education Commission Key Project (KJZD-M202300301, KJZD-K202200302, KJQN201900310); 2022 Chongqing Postgraduate Research Innovation Project (CYS22307); Chongqing Innovative Research Group (CXQT21033); Southwest University of Political Science and Law Research Project (2018XZQN-31, 2018XZQN-33, 2021XZNDJDZD-13, 2022-XZWTYB04).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134348.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rui Huang is a professor at the Criminal Investigation School of Southwest University of Political Science and Law. She works on illicit drug detection, fingerprint imaging and has already achieved a series of practices in these areas. Related research achievements are published in many domestic and foreign academic journals.
Yi Zhou is a graduate student of Southwest University of Political Science and Law. His current research is illicit drug detection.
Jiaxiang Hu is a graduate student of University of Electronic Science and Technology of China. His current research is artificial intelligence data processing.
An Peng is a graduate student of Southwest University of Political Science and Law. His current research is illicit drug detection.

Weihao Hu is a professor of University of Electronic Science and Technology of China. Selected by the National "Young Thousand Talents Program" and Sichuan Province "Thousand Talents Program", he has long been devoted to the research of artificial intelligence and renewable energy generation, and has rich experience in international cooperation.
